title: Playwright Rendering, Isolation, Readiness, and Dynamic-DOM Evidence · Scenario Checkpoint
summary: A release reviewer compares near-miss TypeScript decisions for playwright rendering, isolation, readiness, and dynamic-dom evidence and accepts only compiler plus runtime changed-case evidence, explicit non-claims, and named network, DNS, browser, accessibility, security, load, legal-review, and production transfer gates. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for isolated evidence-driven rendering adapter. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-…
objectives: Render only when static evidence cannot satisfy an authorized stakeholder requirement and retain the decision cause.
Create bounded incognito-like BrowserContexts with explicit storage, permission, download, service-worker, locale, viewport, and close policy.
Wait for application-specific stable evidence under time and request budgets instead of treating network idle as universal readiness.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Render Necessity · changed case · Quiz Render Necessity
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz TypeScript team handles case 1700 during an origin-fairness and event-loop capacity rehearsal. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove necessity, context isolation, readiness, browser network policy, provenance, and closure; browser TypeScript remains deterministic and free of Node, network, filesystem, browser, and host effects; then change one duplicate, accessibility state, axe result, resource budget, browser readiness, or manual-review condition without overclaiming. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Render only when static evidence cannot satisfy an authorized stakeholder requirement and retain the decision cause.

stimulus.title: Rendered Provenance failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: Rendered HTML alone explains the browser state that produced it.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Bind browser version, context policy, viewport, actions, DOM digest, screenshot or trace identity, console errors, and closure evidence.

options.text: Run the boundary case, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.
Accept the result because visible output is the highest authority. That claim does not survive the diagnose the hidden failure evidence boundary.

For diagnose the hidden failure, fetch one convenient page, omit owner authorization, source and emitted revision, URL and robots policy, runtime admission, stream and Promise ownership, Cheerio and browser differences, accessibility states, changed fixtures, recovery, and controlled Node transfer, then accept plausible objects as TypeScript crawler proof.

Write the quiz evidence note for Rendered Provenance: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A quiz TypeScript team handles case 9463 during a hostile redirect, stream, and checkpoint drill. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove necessity, context isolation, readiness, browser network policy, provenance, and closure; every Promise, stream, listener, timer, socket, file, browser context, and report sink needs one cleanup owner; then change one duplicate, accessibility state, axe result, resource budget, browser readiness, or manual-review condition without overclaiming. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Bind browser version, context policy, viewport, actions, DOM digest, screenshot or trace identity, console errors, and closure evidence.
